narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 36, lower-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest 10th percentile based on grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success using patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 67% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough that it tipped the decision to reject your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the strongest in pushing the model toward expecting failure. Most influential was your Year 3 school decile in the bottom 10%, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar struggles ahead. Next came your LSAT score of 36, which is close to average but pulled in the opposite direction, suggesting it helped your case by showing solid reasoning potential compared to lower scorers. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% versus the usual 5th or 6th, added further weight toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum. While factors like your full-time plans aligned with the vast majority of students who pursue that path, they didn't sway the balance much here. Overall, your grades placed you below typical admits, whose stronger deciles and GPAs (averaging 3.21) correlate with bar passers in the model's view. This doesn't define your potential—many from similar backgrounds, including Hispanic students, succeed by addressing these gaps. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your undergrad record through post-bac courses or a master's, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after targeted improvements could flip the model's outlook, and schools often value persistence in applicants like you. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
